finding: chooseSemaphore helper uses Semaphore.make() which returns Effect<Semaphore>, not Semaphore. The plan shows `s = Semaphore.make(item.maxConcurrency)` which would not type-check — Semaphore.make returns Effect.Effect<Semaphore>. Must use Semaphore.makeUnsafe(item.maxConcurrency) instead, since chooseSemaphore is a synchronous function returning Semaphore, not an Effect generator.
repair_path: Replace `Semaphore.make(item.maxConcurrency)` with `Semaphore.makeUnsafe(item.maxConcurrency)` in the chooseSemaphore helper. Also need to add `makeUnsafe` to the Effect destructured import in tools.ts (or import `{ Semaphore }` separately).

finding: Semaphore.availablePermits() does not exist in Effect v4 Semaphore API. The Semaphore interface only exposes take, release, releaseAll, resize, withPermit[s]. No read-only getter for current available permits exists. Span enrichment with semaphore availability count as described is impossible with the public API.
repair_path: Remove the `"semaphore.available"` span attribute from the plan, OR wrap Semaphore with a tracking layer (Ref<number> for available permits) that updates on take/release calls.

finding: `retry?: number` is added to the Tool.Def interface but never consumed in tools.ts. No retry logic is implemented. This is dead surface area on the public interface — any tool setting `retry: 3` would silently ignore it, creating false expectations. Worse, external consumers (plugin tools) could set this field expecting it to work and get no effect.
repair_path: Remove `retry?: number` from the Def interface in this lane. File a follow-up issue to add retry as a separate lane with full implementation in tools.ts.

finding: MCP section type mismatch. The plan says 'Replace semaphore.withPermit(...) with chooseSemaphore(item).withPermit(...) in both built-in and MCP sections.' But MCP tools are not Tool.Def — they have a different structure with 'key' as identifier, no 'cacheable' field, and no 'maxConcurrency' field. chooseSemaphore checks item.cacheable and item.maxConcurrency which don't exist on MCP tool objects, causing a TypeScript type error.
repair_path: For MCP section, either: (1) keep using the global writeSemaphore unconditionally, (2) type-check items before passing to chooseSemaphore, or (3) create a separate chooseMcpSemaphore that maps by key and defaults to writeSemaphore.

finding: No effect timeout error enrichment. When Effect.timeout fires, the error is a generic Cause.TimeoutError with message 'Timed out' and no tool name, tool ID, or arguments context. The Effect.catch handler passes this to Execution.publishFailed and re-fails it. A developer debugging timeout issues sees no indication of WHICH tool timed out.
repair_path: Wrap the timeout error with Effect.catchAll or Effect.mapError before it reaches the existing catch handler. Convert TimeoutError to a typed error that includes tool ID and timeout duration: e.g., `new ToolTimeoutError({ tool: item.id, timeout: item.timeout })`.

finding: The per-tool semaphore Map (perToolSemaphores) has unbounded growth if MCP tools change dynamically. resolve() is called per-request (lines 1387 of prompt.ts), so the Map is scoped to one request — no cross-request leak. However, within a request, if MCP tools() returns different keys across calls within the same resolve() (unlikely but possible), the Map accumulates stale entries. Not a blocking issue but worth documenting.
repair_path: Consider using a WeakRef-based approach or moving per-tool semaphores to a Map that is cleaned up after each resolve() call completes (it already is, since resolve clones the Map into closures, and the Map variable is local). No action needed for current scope — this finding is informational.
